# Claude DESTROYS OpenClaw, Here's Why...

**Channel**: Julian Goldie SEO
**Duration**: 17:15
**Language**: English (auto-generated)
**URL**: https://youtube.com/watch?v=bC_0h1C7or4
**Transcribed**: 2026-03-29 20:03

---

## Organized Notes

**Head-to-Head Comparison: Claude Cowork vs OpenClaw**

- **Julian Goldie** runs both OpenClaw and Claude Cowork side by side to compare on multiple dimensions
- Overall verdict: **Claude Cowork wins for 99% of users** -- faster setup, better UI, lower cost, more reliable
- OpenClaw still has advantages for **power users who want model flexibility and customization**

**Organization and UI**

- **Cowork**: Projects are cleanly separated with visible chat history, scheduled tasks, and run history per project. UI is intuitive and well-organized
- **OpenClaw**: Chat history is harder to navigate. You could use separate Discord channels for projects but that fragments the experience. The schedule section uses "cron jobs" terminology that non-technical users won't understand
- Cowork's **three-mode interface** (Chat, Cowork, Code) acts as a "super app" -- easy to switch between coding, agentic work, and casual chat with hotkeys (Cmd+2)

**Computer Use / Browser Automation**

- Same task given to both: navigate to a specific website and interact with content
- **OpenClaw refused** to complete the task and struggled with browser automation
- **Cowork completed it** -- took screenshots, navigated Chrome, and executed the task without issues
- Cowork is "far more effective at computer use than OpenClaw"

**Cost**

- **OpenClaw**: Per-token API billing. Julian had days spending **$38 in a single day** on Claude Opus API tokens through OpenClaw
- **Cowork**: Fixed monthly subscription (Pro plan). Predictable costs, no token billing surprises
- This is the same point Nick Saraev made in his Dispatch review -- Anthropic **subsidizes the subscription** at 4-8x cheaper than raw API pricing

**Phone Connectivity (Dispatch)**

- **Cowork**: Scan a QR code from the Dispatch section and you're connected. Done
- **OpenClaw**: Requires configuring API tokens for Telegram integration -- get a bot API key, get a token key, get a pairing code. "A lot more messing around"

**App Connections / Tools**

- **Cowork**: Click the plus sign, log into WordPress (or whatever tool), and it's connected
- **OpenClaw**: Browse the skills store, download skills, plug in APIs, provide documentation. More flexible but more work

**Where OpenClaw Still Wins**

- **Model flexibility**: Switch between any AI model (not just Sonnet/Opus). Can test new models immediately when they launch
- **Customization**: Open source ecosystem with projects like **Nerve** that can be set up in 2 minutes for custom mission control
- **Early access to innovation**: OpenClaw gets new agent capabilities before Cowork because Anthropic has more guardrails and slower release cycles
- **Open ecosystem**: "You can change the dials on OpenClaw in a way you probably never could with Claude Cowork"

**Key Recommendation**

- For **99% of people**: use Claude Cowork -- they want outputs, not customization
- OpenClaw is for the **1% of power users** who want full control over models, infrastructure, and tooling
- Claude's trajectory is **rapidly improving** (Cowork, Dispatch, scheduled tasks, auto mode in Code) while OpenClaw has plateaued with mostly "technical and security improvements"
- If you want a cloud computer with an AI agent, use **Perplexity Computer** or **Manus Computer** instead of buying a Mac Mini for OpenClaw

---

## What This Means for Your Work

Julian's comparison reinforces that you're on the right platform. Your Claude Max subscription gives you Cowork + Code + Dispatch -- all three layers -- at a fixed cost. His $38/day OpenClaw API bill would be devastating at the volume of legal research, guidebook generation, and transcript processing you do.

The most actionable insight: **Cowork's project organization model**. Julian shows how separate projects with their own chat history and scheduled tasks keep work organized. You could create Cowork projects for your major workstreams: one for OOBC policy work, one for MoroTech/MoroAcademy, one for legislative research, one for transcriptions. Each would have its own context and history.

The OpenClaw advantages Julian mentions (model flexibility, open ecosystem, faster innovation access) don't apply to you -- you're already committed to Claude's ecosystem and your 129-skill library is built around it.

### How This Can Improve Your Claude Skills and Workflows

**Project-based Cowork organization.** Julian's demo of separate projects with visible histories maps to your workstreams. Setting up dedicated Cowork projects for your major areas (OOBC, MoroTech, legislative research) would prevent context bleeding and make it easier to resume work.

**Scheduled tasks in Cowork.** Julian shows the schedule section. Your `/schedule` skill already handles cron jobs in Claude Code -- but Cowork's scheduled tasks are accessible from Dispatch (phone). You could schedule recurring tasks like: daily news monitoring for Bangsamoro legislative updates, weekly content performance checks for MoroTech, or periodic vault maintenance.

**Computer use for document processing.** Julian demonstrates Cowork's browser automation for navigating websites. This could replace some of your Playwright-based workflows for simpler tasks -- Cowork's computer use is built-in and requires no setup, though your Playwright skills give you more control for complex automation.
